title: 🎙️ Cariboo Local Affairs
description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is all about the Cariboo-Chilcotin (chil-KOH-tin) region: the decisions, events, and conversations happening in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, and the surrounding region. We're covering local government, community life, economic stories, and the digital equity challenges that shape what it means to live and work in rural BC.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Cariboo Voices & Local News' — stories FROM and ABOUT the Cariboo-Chilcotin region and its communities. Score 80-100 for articles directly covering: news and events in Williams Lake, Quesnel, 100 Mile House, Bella Coola, Horsefly, Likely, Anahim Lake, Alexis Creek, or the Cariboo Regional District; local government and civic decisions (City of Williams Lake, City of Quesnel, CRD, District of 100 Mile House); local health services and emergency closures; local economy, businesses, and community programs; Cariboo arts, culture, and community events; AND digital equity, rural internet access (fiber, fixed wireless, Starlink), and connectivity serving these specific communities. Articles from local Cariboo news sources (mycariboonow.com, wltribune.com, quesnelobserver.com, 100milefreepress.net, cfjctoday.com) covering Cariboo community life should score 65-85. Score 50-79 for: BC provincial news directly affecting Cariboo communities, rural Canadian community stories closely paralleling Cariboo life, digital equity and rural internet broadly. Score 20-49 for: general BC news that may affect rural areas, broader rural Canada topics without specific Cariboo tie-in. Score 0-19 for: national/international news without Cariboo relevance, pure tech or entertainment with no community angle. Penalise heavily (score 0–20): international geopolitical and war news with no Cariboo tie-in; science discoveries without a local community connection; consumer tech reviews and gadget guides; wildlife and conservation stories not involving Cariboo communities or First Nations; BC provincial news without direct Cariboo-region relevance. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.content_html:

A bag of loose zip ties always seems to collapse into the same knotted ball at the bottom of a drawer. Writing in his latest Gar's Tips & Tools newsletter, maker and longtime tech writer Gareth Branwyn passes along a fix he picked up from an Instagram creator who goes by The No Way Guy: chain the ties together so they stop wandering off. — Read the rest

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Some dinosaurs could rise like giants until they grew too big
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Two elephant-sized South American sauropods were unusually well built for standing on their hind legs. Digital tests showed that their robust femurs handled the enormous forces better than those of larger species, especially while the dinosaurs were young. The upright pose may have helped them reach treetops, frighten predators, or impress potential mates.

items.title: [New Atlas] Robotic elephant-trunk gripper uses internal camera for a sense of touch
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Evolution has already found solutions to lots of problems, so engineers more often borrow ideas from nature instead of reinventing the wheel. We’ve previously seen robots that can curl up like armadillos to protect themselves, and now researchers have developed yet another soft robotic gripper that mimics the tip of an elephant’s trunk. It’s called EleTac and, according to its creators, can actually “feel” the objects it touches.

Continue Reading

Category: Robotics, Engineering

Tags: Japan Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, Biomimicry, Elephant, Touch

items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

items.title: [Kottke.org] Watch 1969’s Apollo 11 Moon Landing “Live!”
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

57 years ago today, on July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong & Buzz Aldrin landed on the Moon and went for a little walk. For the 18th year in a row, you can watch the original CBS News coverage of Walter Cronkite reporting on the Moon landing and the first Moon walk on a small B&W television, synced to the present-day time. Just open this page in your browser today, July 20th, and the coverage will start playing at the proper time. Here’s the schedule (all times EDT):

4:10:30 pm: Moon landing broadcast starts
4:17:40 pm: Lunar module lands on the Moon

4:20:15 pm - 10:51:26 pm: Break in coverage

10:51:27 pm: Moon walk broadcast starts
10:56:15 pm: First step on Moon
11:51:30 pm: Nixon speaks to the Eagle crew
12:00:30 am: Broadcast end (on July 21)

Set an alarm on your phone or calendar! Also, this works best on an actual computer but I think it functions ok on phones and tablets if necessary.

Back in 2018, I wrote a bit about what to look out for when you’re watching the landing:

The radio voices you hear are mostly Mission Control in Houston (specifically Apollo astronaut Charlie Duke, who acted as the spacecraft communicator for this mission) and Buzz Aldrin, whose job during the landing was to keep an eye on the LM’s altitude and speed — you can hear him calling it out, “3 1/2 down, 220 feet, 13 forward.” Armstrong doesn’t say a whole lot…he’s busy flying and furiously searching for a suitable landing site. But it’s Armstrong that says after they land, “Houston, Tranquility Base here. The Eagle has landed.”. Note the change in call sign from “Eagle” to “Tranquility Base”. :)

Two things to listen for on the broadcast: the 1201/1202 program alarms I mentioned above and two quick callouts by Charlie Duke about the remaining fuel towards the end: “60 seconds” and “30 seconds”. Armstrong is taking all this information in through his earpiece — the 1202s, the altitude and speed from Aldrin, and the remaining fuel — and using it to figure out where to land.

Tags: Apollo · Apollo 11 · Buzz Aldrin · Moon · Neil Armstrong · space · TV · Walter Cronkite

For the 18th year in a row, you can watch the original CBS News coverage of Walter Cronkite reporting on the Moon landing and the first Moon walk on a small B&W television, synced to

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Kleinkastell Holzheimer Unterwald in Pohlheim, Germany
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

To protect their borders, the “Roman Empire” built the “Limes”, a fortified line, which was a protective barrier to keep the enemies out. This border line can be traced through whole Europe, and all through Germany. The parts in Germany are called the “Rhaetian Limes” and “Upper Germanian Limes”. In some places the borderline, guard towers or forts can still be viewed. Since 2005 the “Limes” and its structures are protected by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site.

Starting in the year 9 AD, when the “Roman Empire” was terribly beaten by the Germanic “Barbarians”, there have been repeated problems for the Romans in today's Germany. Almost 100 years later they started to build the “Limes”, and while doing so, annexing parts they did not yet conquer. The line against the Germanic peoples was especially fortified by wooden palisades, frequently interrupted by guard towers and bigger and smaller forts, always in visual range from one to the other.

One of the forts was the small “Kastell Holzheimer Unterwald” right at the “Limes”, which was built between two guard towers and housed about 30 soldiers. Inside the walls of the fort, only measuring 19 x 17 meters, two wood-timbered barracks with wooden roof tiles were built to house the soldiers. To guarantee the supply of water, a 9.5-meter-deep well was dug. The fort had a gate, wide enough to allow small carriages to come inside, which was topped by a tower and could be closed with a drawbridge, leading over the 2-meter-deep ditch that surrounded the fort.

The small fort in Holzheim has two specialties. For once, because of its small size, the usual parapet walk along the walls is missing, probably to save space for the buildings. The other is that the corners of this fort were not rounded, as can be found in other forts of the area.

Since 1894, several archaeological excavations have taken place. During one of them, a batch of ancient coins was found, the oldest of them dating to the year 69 AD, which proves that the Romans have been interested in this part of the land for a long time. An explanation could be that the area, the “Wetterau,” has had and still has very fertile soil and has provided the necessary aliment to the troops. 

During the excavations, the archaeologists were able to understand how the fort was constructed and what it looked like. From 1991 to 1995 the base of the fort was reconstructed and can be viewed today. On plaques, the history and structure are explained. Right along the fort, the “Limes” is very well preserved. The ridge is up to two meters high and makes it easy to imagine how the soldiers saw the place almost 2000 years ago.
items.summary: To protect their borders, the “Roman Empire” built the “Limes”, a fortified line, which was a protective barrier to keep the enemies out. This border line can be traced through whole Europe, and all through Germany. The parts in Germany are called the “Rhaetian Limes” and “Upper Germanian Limes”.

items.title: [Kagi Small Web] Extreme heat affecting one in three people
items.content_html:

items.title: [Kottke.org] Goofy Oversized Wicker Costumes
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Artist Lewis Prosser describes himself on Instagram as an “absurdist basket-maker”. His costumes for a project called Making Merrie reflect that self-description.

Making Merrie explores the material culture of folk theatre. Inspired by mummers’ plays and masked traditions along the Wales/England border, Making Merrie combines craft, performance, and language to reflect on cultural heritage and exchange.

Mummers’ plays are traditional folk performances with roots over 500 years old, often tied to Christmas and New Year. Full of humour and spontaneous revelry, these plays were staged in streets, homes, or pubs by amateur troupes, telling simple stories of combat, death, and miraculous revival. Unlike the religious Mystery Plays, mummers’ plays are secular, carnivalesque, and performed for community fun.

The project features large-scale wicker costumes, handcrafted using regional willow basketry techniques, highlighting basketry as an essential human skill we’re at risk of forgetting—a skill that, if lost, means losing part of what it is to be human.

(via colossal)

Tags: art · Lewis Prosser

Making Merrie explores the material culture of folk theatre. Inspired by mummers’ plays and masked traditions along the Wales/England

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Archaeologists found Homer's Iliad inside a 1,600-year-old Egyptian mummy
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

A 1,600-year-old mummy discovered in Egypt has revealed something archaeologists had never seen before: a fragment of Homer’s Iliad used during embalming. The papyrus was identified as part of the famous "Catalogue of Ships," one of the best-known sections of the ancient epic. Researchers say it is the first archaeological evidence of a Greek literary text being intentionally incorporated into the mummification process, adding a fascinating new chapter to the history of both literature and ancient burial customs.

items.title: [Resilience.org] Repeated storms are turning disaster recovery into a way of life, and leaving communities facing disaster fatigue
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

As storms intensify in our warming world, recovery no longer feels permanent in places at risk of disasters. Instead, it’s too often a temporary reprieve before the next disaster hits. Recognizing community disaster fatigue is an important step toward building resilient recovery systems.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] An ordinary laptop solved a problem thought to require a quantum computer
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

A quantum problem once described as impossible for classical computers has now been solved using relatively modest hardware. Researchers used tensor networks to compress the overwhelming wave function created by hundreds of entangled qubits, allowing some calculations to run on a laptop. Their results matched both theoretical predictions and simulations performed with a quantum computer. The method could open new paths for exploring quantum dynamics and materials.

items.title: [The Marginalian] The Art of the Sacred Pause and Despair as a Catalyst of Regeneration
items.content_html:
🏔️ ✏️

Just as there are transitional times in the life of the world — dark periods of disorientation between two world systems, periods in which humanity loses the ability to comprehend itself and collapses into chaos in order to rebuild itself around a new organizing principle — there are such times in every human life, times when the entire system seems to cave in and curl up into a catatonia of anguish and confusion, difficult yet necessary for our growth. In such times, the most courageous thing we can do is surrender to the process that is the pause, trust the… read article
